longDescription:
Jam-e Jam - The World-Reflecting Goblet of Persian Legend The Cup of Jamshid (Jam-e Jam) is one of the most celebrated magical objects in Persian
                    mythology and literature. This legendary goblet, filled with the elixir of immortality,
                    possessed the miraculous power to reflect the entire world within its depths. By gazing
                    into the cup, its possessor could see everything happening anywhere in the seven regions
                    of the earth.

sources.author: Ferdowsi
sources.title: Shahnameh
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Jamshid and the Cup, detailing its powers and significance.

sources.author: Arthur George Warner and Edmond Warner
sources.title: The Sháhnáma of Firdausí
sources.description: An English translation of the Shahnameh, providing access to the epic for English-speaking audiences.

powers: Reveals all the world and its secrets
shortDescription: Jam-e Jam - The World-Reflecting Goblet of Persian Legend
name: Cup of Jamshid

extendedContent.content:
The Cup of Jamshid (Jam-e Jam) is one of the most celebrated magical objects in Persian
                    mythology and literature. This legendary goblet, filled with the elixir of immortality,
                    possessed the miraculous power to reflect the entire world within its depths. By gazing
                    into the cup, its possessor could see everything happening anywhere in the seven regions
                    of the earth.
                
                
                    The cup belonged to the legendary King Jamshid, one of the greatest rulers of the mythical
                    Pishdadian dynasty. In Jamshid's reign, humanity lived in a golden age of prosperity and
                    peace, and the cup represented the king's divine wisdom and far-seeing judgment.
extendedContent.title: Description and Nature

extendedContent.content:
King Jamshid
                
                    Jamshid was the fourth king of the world in Persian mythology, ruling during humanity's
                    golden age. Under his reign, which lasted 700 years, there was no disease, death, or
                    suffering. He is credited with discovering medicine, dividing society into classes,
                    teaching metalworking, and establishing Nowruz (the Persian New Year).
                
                
                    However, Jamshid's downfall came through pride. After centuries of glorious rule, he
                    began to believe his achievements were his own rather than gifts from Ahura Mazda
                    (the supreme god). His divine farr (kingly glory) departed, and he was eventually
                    overthrown and killed by the serpent-shouldered tyrant Zahhak.
                

                
                    "The cup of Jamshid wherein the world was reflected - seek it in the ruins of the heart."
                    — Hafez, Persian Sufi poet
                

                Sufi Interpretation
                
                    In Sufi poetry, the Cup of Jamshid became a powerful mystical symbol. The cup represents
                    the heart of the spiritual seeker, which when purified becomes a mirror reflecting
                    divine truth. The world-revealing power of the cup symbolizes enlightenment and the
                    direct perception of reality that comes through spiritual discipline.
                
                
                    Poets like Hafez and Rumi used the Cup of Jamshid as a metaphor for the soul's journey
                    toward God, suggesting that the true cup is not an external object but the polished
                    heart of the mystic who has transcended ego and worldly attachments.
extendedContent.title: Mythology and Legend

extendedContent.content:
The Cup of Jamshid holds immense importance in Persian cultural identity. It appears
                    throughout the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi, the national epic of Iran, and
                    pervades Persian poetry from the medieval period to the present day.
                
                
                    The image of the world-reflecting cup has been compared to similar objects in other
                    traditions: the Holy Grail, the mirror of Galadriel, and various scrying devices. Some
                    scholars have suggested connections between Persian and Celtic vessel mythology, though
                    direct influence is difficult to establish.
extendedContent.title: Cultural Significance

symbolism:
The Cup of Jamshid holds immense importance in Persian cultural identity. It appears
                    throughout the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi, the national epic of Iran, and
                    pervades Persian poetry from the medieval period to the present day.
                
                
                    The image of the world-reflecting cup has been compared to similar objects in other
                    traditions: the Holy Grail, the mirror of Galadriel, and various scrying devices. Some
                    scholars have suggested connections between Persian and Celtic vessel mythology, though
                    direct influence is difficult to establish.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Cup of Jamshid continues to be referenced in Persian literature, art, and popular culture as a symbol of wisdom, foresight, and the ephemeral nature of power. It is often invoked in poetry to represent the pursuit of knowledge and the longing for a golden age.

description:
The Cup of Jamshid, also known as Jam-e Jam, is a significant artifact in Persian mythology, embodying themes of divination, power, and cosmic reflection. Often described as a goblet or cup filled with the elixir of immortality, it is primarily associated with Jamshid, the fourth king of the Pishdadian dynasty in the Shahnameh (Book of Kings) by Ferdowsi. This mythical cup wasn't merely a drinking vessel; it possessed the extraordinary ability to reveal all the secrets of the world to its owner. By gazing into the cup, Jamshid could foresee the future, understand the past, and observe events occurring in distant lands.

The Jam-e Jam is not merely a tool for clairvoyance but also a symbol of legitimate kingship and divine favor. Its possession signified not only power but also wisdom and the responsibility to rule justly. Over time, the cup's symbolism evolved, becoming linked to concepts of spiritual enlightenment and the unveiling of inner truths. Later interpretations associated it with the human heart, suggesting that true knowledge and understanding come from within. The cup's power is not inherent in the object itself, but rather a reflection of the purity and wisdom of its wielder. Its disappearance from the Shahnameh marks the decline of Jamshid's reign, attributed to his hubris and loss of divine grace, underscoring the moral lesson that even the most powerful are subject to the consequences of their actions. The Cup of Jamshid remains a potent symbol in Persian culture, representing the pursuit of knowledge, the ephemeral nature of power, and the importance of maintaining humility.

associations.name: Divination
associations.significance: The cup allows its owner to foresee the future and understand the present.

associations.name: Kingship
associations.significance: Possession of the cup signifies legitimate rule and divine favor.

associations.name: Cosmic Reflection
associations.significance: The cup reflects the entire universe, revealing its secrets.

associations.name: Immortality
associations.significance: The elixir contained within grants or symbolizes eternal life.

associations.name: Wisdom
associations.significance: Using the cup requires wisdom and understanding to interpret its visions.

associations.name: Hubris
associations.significance: The loss of the cup is associated with Jamshid's pride and downfall.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both provide prophetic visions and guidance, though one is a place and the other an object.
Both are sacred vessels with the power to grant boons and reveal spiritual truths.
A source of wisdom and knowledge, though requiring sacrifice to access its insights.

subtitle: A divine artifact of immense power and profound insight.

wielders.name: Jamshid
wielders.context: The primary owner of the cup, who used it to rule wisely for centuries.

wielders.name: Kay Khosrow
wielders.context: Another king who, in some traditions, is associated with possessing similar divinely-ordained artifacts.
